summary:
The presence of multiple permeating lesions involving all bones strongly suggests an underlying hematologic or systemic disease. Among available choices, Histioctysis X (now recognized as Langerhans cell histiocytosis) best explains these findings due to its well-documented association with multifocal, lytic, and widely distributed bone destruction—particularly in pediatric patients.

The correct answer is therefore $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

Okay, now let’s evaluate Option D: Osteopetrosis  
Osteopetrosis leads to increased density/bony sclerosis instead of perforation/permeation. It presents with dense bones, fractures easily, cranial nerve compression symptoms, etc.—but NOT typically shows “permeating” or “destructive” lesions. In fact, it results in **increased boney mass**, opposite direction of lesion described here ("lesions" implies loss). So clearly inconsistent with 'permeating' nature mentioned.
Thus ruled out immediately based upon pathophysiology mismatch.

Final evaluation:

| Condition | Bone Lesion Pattern |
|--|--|
| Neuroblastoma | Possible limited spread; no strong link to universal permissive bone changes |
| Osteosarcoma Mets | Very uncommon & usually lung-first; unlikely for total skeletal diffusion |
| Histiocytosis X / LCH | Classic presentation = multibone lytic/destructive lesions |
| Osteopetrosis | Sclerotic/dense bones—not lytic nor permeating |

Therefore, only option matching both distribution AND type of lesion is **Histiocytosis X/LCH**.

Answer remains confidently boxed at $ \boxed{\text{C} } $

---
